topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white walls of the clinic felt like a physical weight pressing down on me. I clutched the worn strap of my messenger bag, the cheap fabric digging into my shoulder. Every time Dr. Thorne looked up, a familiar, unwelcome heat bloomed in my chest, a tightening that made my breath catch. He was sitting across from me, his expression calm, almost unnervingly gentle. It was Mark. Mark, who used to dismantle my confidence with a flick of his wrist and a sneer that tasted like sour milk. Now, he was dissecting my fractured past, his voice a low, measured balm. I kept staring at the polished wood of the table, tracing the grain with my eyes, trying to find an escape route in the pattern. I needed this to stop, needed the suffocating pressure to lift, but the proximity to him made my hands tremble uncontrollably.

[story 2]

My palms were slick, clammy against the cool metal of the intake form. I kept running the same mental replay: the echoing laughter, the sharp words that had carved trenches into my self-esteem. When Mark finally looked up, his eyes held a strange, knowing depth that made my throat constrict. He didn't offer platitudes; he just observed, cataloging the frantic way I shifted in my chair, the way I swallowed hard before answering. "Tell me what you're holding back," he prompted, his tone devoid of judgment, which somehow made the scrutiny worse. I wanted to shatter the silence, to scream the years of resentment and pain out of my system, but the words wouldn't form. All that escaped was a shallow, ragged exhale, a sound that felt too small for the cavern of feeling inside me.

[story 3]

I paced the small waiting room, the floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. Each tick of the clock seemed to mock my inertia. When the door finally opened, and Mark walked in, the air in the room seemed to thin out, leaving me feeling exposed and brittle. He offered a small, almost imperceptible nod. That nod. It was the same gesture he used to give me before delivering a particularly cutting remark in the hallway. Now, it was laced with a strange, professional sympathy that twisted my stomach into a knot. I sank into the chair, my posture collapsing inward, shoulders hunching as if bracing for an impact. I looked up at him, my gaze darting everywhere but his face, trying to gauge if this new dynamic was going to break me further or somehow, impossibly, begin to mend the damage.
